message:
feat(packs): add AWS API Gateway pack and GCS storage pack

- Add apigateway.aws pack with protection for REST API and HTTP API operations
  - 18 destructive patterns covering delete operations for APIs, resources,
    routes, stages, integrations, authorizers, etc.
  - 27 safe patterns for get/list operations
  - 15 unit tests
- Add storage.gcs pack for Google Cloud Storage protection (parallel work)
- Fix filesystem.rs macro calls to use string literals instead of constants
- Fix RmFlagState struct to remove Copy trait (Range doesn't impl Copy)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-6ao","title":"Feature: Codecov Cloud Integration for Coverage Trend Tracking","description":"# Feature: Codecov Cloud Integration

## Background

DCG currently generates coverage reports using `cargo-llvm-cov` and stores them as CI artifacts. While this provides point-in-time coverage data, it lacks:

1. **Historical trend tracking** - Can't see if coverage is improving or declining over time
2. **PR coverage diffs** - No automatic comments showing what lines a PR adds/removes from coverage
3. **Coverage badges** - No README badge showing current coverage status
4. **Cross-branch comparison** - Can't compare feature branch coverage against main

CASS (coding_agent_session_search) has Codecov integration that provides all of these. This feature brings DCG to parity.

## Why This Matters for DCG

DCG enforces strict coverage thresholds:
- 70% overall minimum
- 80% for src/evaluator.rs (core decision logic)
- 80% for src/hook.rs (output formatting)

These thresholds are enforced but not tracked over time. With Codecov:
- We can see coverage trends (are we improving?)
- PRs automatically show coverage impact
- New code that drops coverage is immediately visible
- Team can set coverage targets and track progress

## Technical Approach

### Codecov Setup
1. Codecov is free for open source repositories
2. No token required for public repos (DCG is public)
3. Uses GitHub App integration for PR comments

### CI Changes
Modify `.github/workflows/ci.yml` coverage job:

```yaml
- name: Upload to Codecov
  uses: codecov/codecov-action@v4
  with:
    files: lcov.info
    fail_ci_if_error: false  # Don't fail build if upload fails
    verbose: true
```

### Coverage Configuration
Create `codecov.yml` in repo root:

```yaml
coverage:
  status:
    project:
      default:
        target: 70%
        threshold: 2%
    patch:
      default:
        target: 80%  # New code should be well-tested

comment:
  layout: "reach,diff,flags,tree"
  behavior: default
  require_changes: true

ignore:
  - "tests/**"
  - "benches/**"
  - "fuzz/**"
```

## Implementation Tasks

1. **Add Codecov upload to CI** - Modify coverage job
2. **Create codecov.yml config** - Define coverage targets and behavior
3. **Add coverage badge to README** - Show current status
4. **Verify PR comments work** - Test on a real PR

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Coverage uploads to Codecov on every push/PR
- [ ] PR comments show coverage diff
- [ ] README displays coverage badge
- [ ] Codecov dashboard shows historical trends
- [ ] No CI failures from Codecov (fail_ci_if_error: false)

## Considerations

- **Token**: Not required for public repos, but can add CODECOV_TOKEN secret for reliability
- **Coverage format**: Using LCOV (lcov.info) which Codecov supports natively
- **Existing thresholds**: Keep our enforced thresholds in CI; Codecov is additive visibility
- **Privacy**: Coverage data is public for public repos - this is fine for DCG

## References

- Codecov GitHub Action: https://github.com/codecov/codecov-action

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-6ozg","title":"[TASK] CI/CD integration for pack testing","description":"# CI/CD Integration for Pack Testing

## Scope
Ensure all pack tests run automatically on every PR and provide clear feedback.

## Deliverables

### 1. GitHub Actions Workflow Updates
Update `.github/workflows/ci.yml`:
```yaml
jobs:
  pack-tests:
    name: Pack Unit Tests
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Run pack unit tests
        run: cargo test --package destructive_command_guard packs::
      - name: Upload test results
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: pack-test-results
          path: target/test-results/

  pack-e2e:
    name: Pack E2E Tests
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Run E2E test suite
        run: ./scripts/e2e_pack_tests.sh --all --json-output
      - name: Upload E2E results
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4

  pack-coverage:
    name: Pack Coverage
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Generate coverage report
        run: cargo llvm-cov --package destructive_command_guard
      - name: Check coverage threshold
        run: |
          COVERAGE=$(cargo llvm-cov --json | jq '.coverage')
          if (( $(echo "$COVERAGE < 90" | bc -l) )); then
            echo "Coverage $COVERAGE% below 90% threshold"
            exit 1
          fi
```

### 2. PR Check Requirements
Configure branch protection:
- All pack tests must pass
- Coverage must be >= 90%
- E2E tests must pass
- No new warnings from clippy

### 3. Test Result Reporting
- Generate human-readable test summary in PR comments
- Link to detailed test logs
- Highlight any new test failures

## Logging Requirements
- CI logs show: pack name, test count, pass/fail, duration
- Failed tests show: full command, expected result, actual result
- Performance metrics: time per pack, total test time

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Pack tests run on every PR
- [ ] Coverage threshold enforced
- [ ] Clear failure messages in PR
- [ ] Test artifacts preserved for debugging","notes":"Update (2026-01-09):

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-6sg","title":"Design destructive pattern library structure","description":"# Design Destructive Pattern Library Structure

## Objective

Design the structure and organization of language-specific destructive patterns for heredoc/inline-script scanning.

## Background

Each supported language has its own idioms for destructive operations. We need a well-organized pattern library that:

- is maintainable and extensible
- integrates cleanly with the existing pack system
- has stable rule IDs + metadata for explainability/allowlisting
- is *false-positive resistant* by default

## Guiding principles (important)

- Prefer **high-signal** patterns that map directly to real irreversible operations.
  - Example (high signal): `exec.Command("rm", "-rf", ...)`
  - Example (low signal): `exec.Command($CMD)` (too broad; should be warn-only or out of scope)
- Pattern metadata must be first-class:
  - `pack_id`, `pattern_name` → stable rule ID
  - `severity` (ties into `git_safety_guard-1gt.3.1`)
  - short reason + optional suggestion
- Every pattern addition must include:
  - at least 1 positive fixture
  - at least 1 negative fixture targeting common false positives

## Pattern categories by language (initial)

### Python

- Filesystem: `shutil.rmtree`, `os.remove` / `os.unlink` when clearly recursive or system-path targeted
- Process execution: `os.system("rm -rf ...")`, `subprocess.*(["rm", "-rf", ...])`
- DB strings: obvious `DROP` / `TRUNCATE` when executed

### Shell/Bash

- `rm -rf` (non-temp)
- destructive `git` forms (`reset --hard`, `clean -fd`, etc.)
- destructive pipe targets (e.g., `| bash` from decoded payloads)

### JavaScript/Node

- Filesystem: `fs.rmSync(..., { recursive: true })`, `fs.rmdirSync(..., { recursive: true })`
- Process: `child_process.execSync("rm -rf ...")` or `spawnSync("rm", ["-rf", ...])`

### Ruby

- Filesystem: `FileUtils.rm_rf`, `File.delete` on high-risk paths
- Process: backticks/system/exec with direct destructive payload

### Perl

- `system("rm -rf ...")` / backticks with direct payload
- recursive delete helpers if directly invoked with a literal dangerous target

## Design considerations

### 1. Pattern specification format

Decide between:

- Hardcoded Rust (fast, type-safe)
- External files (flexible, needs loading/validation)
- Hybrid (core hardcoded + optional user extensions)

Whatever we pick must support stable IDs and deterministic ordering.

### 2. Pattern metadata

Each pattern needs:

- stable identity: `(pack_id, pattern_name)`
- language (for heredoc scanning)
- matcher form (regex vs AST query vs ast-grep pattern)
- reason + optional suggestions
- severity
- expected FP risk (notes)

### 3. Pack integration

Decide how heredoc patterns show up in packs:

- `heredoc.<language>` packs, OR
- a single `heredoc` pack with per-language submatchers

The output should still attribute a stable rule ID.

### 4. Contextual patterns

Some matchers require context to stay high-signal:

- process execution calls are only dangerous when the payload is destructive
- filesystem calls are only catastrophic when recursive/system-path targeted

This means many patterns should be designed as *composite matchers* (AST match + string/payload check).

### 5. Allowlisting

Users must be able to allowlist legitimate patterns by stable rule ID (not raw regex), with optional scoping/expiration.

## Deliverables

- Pattern specification format (schema + example)
- Initial pattern inventory per language focused on high-signal destructive operations
- Integration plan with pack system + evaluator + explain output
- Pattern authoring checklist (FP controls + tests)

## Acceptance Criteria

- Produces a clear, consistent pattern metadata schema usable by:
  - hook decision output
  - `dcg explain`
  - `dcg allowlist`
  - `dcg scan`
- Defines a policy that prevents low-signal patterns from becoming default hard denies.
- Defines the minimum test/fixture requirements for adding a pattern.
- Enables stable rule IDs that do not change across builds.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-78a","title":"[PACK] remote.rsync - rsync Pack","description":"# rsync Remote Sync Pack - PROMOTED TO TIER 1

## Why Tier 1 (Critical)
**This pack was promoted from Tier 3 to Tier 1** because `rsync --delete` is statistically one of the most common destructive command mistakes. Analysis of developer surveys, Stack Overflow questions, and incident reports shows this is a top-5 footgun.

## Scope
Implement pattern matching for rsync operations.

## Destructive Patterns
### CRITICAL: --delete variants
- `rsync --delete` - Deletes files at dest not in source
- `rsync --delete-before` - Delete before transfer
- `rsync --delete-during` - Delete during transfer  
- `rsync --delete-after` - Delete after transfer
- `rsync --delete-excluded` - Delete excluded files too
- `rsync --del` - Short alias for --delete

### Direction Mistakes
- Wrong source/dest direction can overwrite important files
- Empty source + --delete = wipes destination

## Safe Patterns
- `rsync -n` / `rsync --dry-run` - Preview only
- `rsync --list-only` - List without transfer
- `rsync -v` - Verbose (for inspection)
- `rsync` without --delete flags - Copy only, no deletion

## Implementation Notes
- Parent category: Promoted to Tier 1 (was remote.*)
- Keywords: rsync
- CRITICAL: Must catch all --delete variants including short forms

## Testing Requirements
- [ ] Unit tests in `src/packs/remote/rsync.rs`
- [ ] E2E tests in `scripts/e2e_tests/rsync.txt`
- [ ] Test all --delete variants (--delete, --del, --delete-before, etc.)
- [ ] Test --dry-run makes --delete safe
- [ ] Test edge cases (--delete in quotes, environment variables)
- [ ] Performance < 500µs

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All --delete variants blocked
- [ ] --dry-run recognized as safe modifier

- CI fails when coverage drops below defined thresholds.
- Coverage summary still attached as artifact / job summary.
- Thresholds documented in the repo (doc or CI comments).

```
Docs: https://dcg.dev/packs/secrets/vault
```

### 4. Feedback Collection

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-woe","title":"Feature: Memory Leak Detection Tests","description":"# Feature: Memory Leak Detection Tests

## Background

DCG runs as a hook on EVERY Bash command executed by Claude Code. In a typical coding session:
- Hundreds to thousands of commands are executed
- Each command invokes DCG
- Memory leaks accumulate across invocations
- Long sessions could exhaust system memory

CASS has dedicated memory leak tests that run in CI to catch leaks early. DCG needs the same.

## Why This Matters for DCG Specifically

### Usage Pattern
```
User starts Claude Code session
  → Claude executes: git status        → DCG invoked
  → Claude executes: ls -la            → DCG invoked
  → Claude executes: cargo build       → DCG invoked
  → ... (repeat 500+ times)            → DCG invoked each time
Session ends after hours
```

Even small leaks (a few KB per invocation) compound:
- 1000 invocations × 1KB leak = 1MB leaked
- Long session with 5000 invocations = 5MB leaked
- Multiple concurrent sessions = problem

### Critical Code Paths

These DCG components process every command and must not leak:

1. **Input parsing** (`hook.rs`)
   - JSON parsing with serde_json
   - String allocation for command extraction

2. **Pattern matching** (`evaluator.rs`)
   - Regex compilation (lazy_static, but still)
   - Match result allocation

3. **Heredoc processing** (`heredoc.rs`)
   - Content extraction
   - Script parsing
   - AST construction

4. **Output generation** (`trace.rs`, `hook.rs`)
   - JSON serialization
   - Colorful warning formatting

## Technical Approach

### Test Infrastructure

Create `tests/memory_tests.rs` with:

```rust
//! Memory leak detection tests
//! 
//! These tests process many inputs and check that memory usage
//! doesn't grow unboundedly. They must run with --test-threads=1
//! to get accurate measurements.

use std::alloc::{GlobalAlloc, Layout, System};
use std::sync::atomic::{AtomicUsize, Ordering};

/// Custom allocator that tracks allocations
struct TrackingAllocator;

static ALLOCATED: AtomicUsize = AtomicUsize::new(0);

unsafe impl GlobalAlloc for TrackingAllocator {
    unsafe fn alloc(&self, layout: Layout) -> *mut u8 {
        ALLOCATED.fetch_add(layout.size(), Ordering::SeqCst);
        System.alloc(layout)
    }
    
    unsafe fn dealloc(&self, ptr: *mut u8, layout: Layout) {
        ALLOCATED.fetch_sub(layout.size(), Ordering::SeqCst);
        System.dealloc(ptr, layout)
    }
}

fn memory_usage() -> usize {
    ALLOCATED.load(Ordering::SeqCst)
}
```

### Test Cases

1. **Hook input parsing** - Parse 1000 JSON inputs, check memory stable
2. **Pattern evaluation** - Evaluate 1000 commands, check memory stable
3. **Heredoc extraction** - Process 1000 heredocs, check memory stable
4. **Full pipeline** - End-to-end 1000 times, check memory stable

### Memory Budget

Tests should assert:
- Memory growth < 1MB over 1000 iterations
- No single iteration leaks > 10KB
- Final memory within 10% of baseline after GC

## Implementation Tasks

1. **Create memory test infrastructure** - Tracking allocator, helpers
2. **Add hook parsing memory test** - JSON input processing
3. **Add pattern matching memory test** - Evaluator path
4. **Add heredoc processing memory test** - Script extraction
5. **Add memory tests to CI workflow** - Dedicated job

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Memory tests exist and pass locally
- [ ] Memory tests run in CI (dedicated job)
- [ ] Tests catch intentionally introduced leaks
- [ ] Memory growth < 1MB over 1000 iterations
- [ ] Tests run with --test-threads=1 for accuracy

## Considerations

### Test Isolation
Memory tests must run sequentially (--test-threads=1) to get accurate measurements. Other tests running concurrently would pollute measurements.

### CI Resources
Memory tests should run on a dedicated CI job to:
- Avoid interference with other tests
- Have predictable resource allocation
- Allow longer timeouts if needed

### Platform Differences
Memory tracking may behave differently on Linux vs macOS. Focus on Linux (CI environment) but tests should pass everywhere.

### False Positives
Some "leaks" are actually intentional caches:
- Lazy static regex patterns (compile once, keep forever)
- Pack pattern registry (loaded once at startup)

Tests should account for expected one-time allocations.

## References

- CASS memory tests: /data/projects/coding_agent_session_search/.github/workflows/bench.yml (memory-tests job)
- Rust tracking allocator: https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/alloc/trait.GlobalAlloc.html","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-09T14:21:21.710163301-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-11T02:48:31.321864332-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-11T02:48:31.321864332-05:00","close_reason":"All dependencies completed: memory test infrastructure (dj8), memory tests (39k), and CI job (8zo) are all closed; tests/memory_tests.rs exists and memory-tests job in CI workflow","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-woe","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-dj8","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T14:25:12.375811198-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-woe","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-39k","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T14:25:12.406752934-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-woe","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-8zo","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T14:25:12.43467007-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-x3g","title":"[CATEGORY] dns.* - DNS Management Pack Suite","description":"# DNS Management Pack Suite

## Overview
DNS is critical infrastructure. Incorrect DNS changes can make services unreachable. Zone deletion or record manipulation affects all users globally.

## Packs to Implement

### dns.cloudflare
- **Why Critical**: Cloudflare manages DNS for millions of domains. Zone/record deletion causes immediate outages.
- **Key Destructive Patterns**:
  - `cloudflare dns delete` (wrangler)
  - `curl -X DELETE api.cloudflare.com/.../dns_records/`
  - `terraform destroy` on cloudflare resources
- **Safe Patterns**: `list`, `show`, GET requests

### dns.route53
- **Why Critical**: AWS Route53 handles DNS for AWS-hosted services. Hosted zone deletion affects all records.
- **Key Destructive Patterns**:
  - `aws route53 delete-hosted-zone`
  - `aws route53 change-resource-record-sets` with DELETE action
  - `aws route53 delete-health-check`
- **Safe Patterns**: `list-`, `get-`, `test-dns-answer`

### dns.generic
- **Why Critical**: Generic DNS tools (dig, host, nslookup, nsupdate) - nsupdate can modify records.
- **Key Destructive Patterns**:
  - `nsupdate` with delete commands
  - Zone transfers to untrusted servers
- **Safe Patterns**: `dig`, `host`, `nslookup` (read-only)

## Rationale
DNS changes propagate globally with TTL delays. A bad change can take hours to fix even after correction. Zone delegation changes can effectively transfer domain control.

## Dependencies
- Depends on: Tier 2 Epic (git_safety_guard-hhh)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Major DNS providers covered
- [ ] API-based changes detected

+//! AWS API Gateway pack - protections for destructive AWS API Gateway operations.
+//!
+//! Covers destructive operations for both REST APIs (APIGateway) and HTTP APIs (APIGatewayV2):
+//! - REST API deletion (`aws apigateway delete-rest-api`)
+//! - Resource deletion (`aws apigateway delete-resource`)
+//! - Method deletion (`aws apigateway delete-method`)
+//! - Stage deletion (`aws apigateway delete-stage`, `aws apigatewayv2 delete-stage`)
+//! - Deployment deletion (`aws apigateway delete-deployment`)
+//! - API key deletion (`aws apigateway delete-api-key`)
+//! - HTTP API deletion (`aws apigatewayv2 delete-api`)
+//! - Route deletion (`aws apigatewayv2 delete-route`)
+//! - Integration deletion (`aws apigatewayv2 delete-integration`)
+
+use crate::packs::{DestructivePattern, Pack, SafePattern};
+use crate::{destructive_pattern, safe_pattern};
+
+/// Create the AWS API Gateway pack.
+#[must_use]
+pub fn create_pack() -> Pack {
+    Pack {
+        id: "apigateway.aws".to_string(),
+        name: "AWS API Gateway",
+        description: "Protects against destructive AWS API Gateway CLI operations for both \
+                      REST APIs and HTTP APIs.",
+        keywords: &["aws", "apigateway", "apigatewayv2"],
+        safe_patterns: create_safe_patterns(),
+        destructive_patterns: create_destructive_patterns(),
+        keyword_matcher: None,
+    }
+}
+
+fn create_safe_patterns() -> Vec<SafePattern> {
+    vec![
+        // REST API (APIGateway) - read operations
+        safe_pattern!(
+            "apigateway-get-rest-api",
+            r"aws\s+apigateway\s+get-rest-api\b"
+        ),
+        safe_pattern!(
+            "apigateway-get-rest-apis",
+            r"aws\s+apigateway\s+get-rest-apis\b"
+        ),
+        safe_pattern!(
+            "apigateway-get-resources",
+            r"aws\s+apigateway\s+get-resources\b"
+        ),
+        safe_pattern!(
+            "apigateway-get-resource",
+            r"aws\s+apigateway\s+get-resource\b"
+        ),
+        safe_pattern!("apigateway-get-method", r"aws\s+apigateway\s+get-method\b"),
+        safe_pattern!("apigateway-get-stages", r"aws\s+apigateway\s+get-stages\b"),
+        safe_pattern!("apigateway-get-stage", r"aws\s+apigateway\s+get-stage\b"),
+        safe_pattern!(
+            "apigateway-get-deployments",
+            r"aws\s+apigateway\s+get-deployments\b"
+        ),
+        safe_pattern!(
+            "apigateway-get-deployment",
+            r"aws\s+apigateway\s+get-deployment\b"
+        ),
+        safe_pattern!(
+            "apigateway-get-api-keys",
+            r"aws\s+apigateway\s+get-api-keys\b"
+        ),
+        safe_pattern!(
+            "apigateway-get-api-key",
+            r"aws\s+apigateway\s+get-api-key\b"
+        ),
+        safe_pattern!(
+            "apigateway-get-authorizers",
+            r"aws\s+apigateway\s+get-authorizers\b"
+        ),
+        safe_pattern!("apigateway-get-models", r"aws\s+apigateway\s+get-models\b"),
+        safe_pattern!(
+            "apigateway-get-usage-plans",
+            r"aws\s+apigateway\s+get-usage-plans\b"
+        ),
+        safe_pattern!(
+            "apigateway-get-domain-names",
+            r"aws\s+apigateway\s+get-domain-names\b"
+        ),
+        // HTTP API (APIGatewayV2) - read operations
+        safe_pattern!("apigatewayv2-get-apis", r"aws\s+apigatewayv2\s+get-apis\b"),
+        safe_pattern!("apigatewayv2-get-api", r"aws\s+apigatewayv2\s+get-api\b"),
+        safe_pattern!(
+            "apigatewayv2-get-routes",
+            r"aws\s+apigatewayv2\s+get-routes\b"
+        ),
+        safe_pattern!(
+            "apigatewayv2-get-route",
+            r"aws\s+apigatewayv2\s+get-route\b"
+        ),
+        safe_pattern!(
+            "apigatewayv2-get-integrations",
+            r"aws\s+apigatewayv2\s+get-integrations\b"
+        ),
+        safe_pattern!(
+            "apigatewayv2-get-integration",
+            r"aws\s+apigatewayv2\s+get-integration\b"
+        ),
+        safe_pattern!(
+            "apigatewayv2-get-stages",
+            r"aws\s+apigatewayv2\s+get-stages\b"
+        ),
+        safe_pattern!(
+            "apigatewayv2-get-stage",
+            r"aws\s+apigatewayv2\s+get-stage\b"
+        ),
+        safe_pattern!(
+            "apigatewayv2-get-authorizers",
+            r"aws\s+apigatewayv2\s+get-authorizers\b"
+        ),
+        safe_pattern!(
+            "apigatewayv2-get-domain-names",
+            r"aws\s+apigatewayv2\s+get-domain-names\b"
+        ),
+        // General AWS help
+        safe_pattern!("apigateway-help", r"aws\s+apigateway\s+(?:help|\-\-help)\b"),
+        safe_pattern!(
+            "apigatewayv2-help",
+            r"aws\s+apigatewayv2\s+(?:help|\-\-help)\b"
+        ),
+    ]
+}
+
+fn create_destructive_patterns() -> Vec<DestructivePattern> {
+    vec![
+        // REST API (APIGateway) - destructive operations
+        destructive_pattern!(
+            "apigateway-delete-rest-api",
+            r"aws\s+apigateway\s+delete-rest-api\b",
+            "aws apigateway delete-rest-api permanently removes a REST API and all its resources."
+        ),
+        destructive_pattern!(
+            "apigateway-delete-resource",
+            r"aws\s+apigateway\s+delete-resource\b",
+            "aws apigateway delete-resource removes an API resource and its methods."
+        ),
+        destructive_pattern!(
+            "apigateway-delete-method",
+            r"aws\s+apigateway\s+delete-method\b",
+            "aws apigateway delete-method removes an HTTP method from a resource."
+        ),
+        destructive_pattern!(
+            "apigateway-delete-stage",
+            r"aws\s+apigateway\s+delete-stage\b",
+            "aws apigateway delete-stage removes a deployment stage from an API."
+        ),
+        destructive_pattern!(
+            "apigateway-delete-deployment",
+            r"aws\s+apigateway\s+delete-deployment\b",
+            "aws apigateway delete-deployment removes a deployment from an API."
+        ),
+        destructive_pattern!(
+            "apigateway-delete-api-key",
+            r"aws\s+apigateway\s+delete-api-key\b",
+            "aws apigateway delete-api-key removes an API key."
+        ),
+        destructive_pattern!(
+            "apigateway-delete-authorizer",
+            r"aws\s+apigateway\s+delete-authorizer\b",
+            "aws apigateway delete-authorizer removes an authorizer from an API."
+        ),
+        destructive_pattern!(
+            "apigateway-delete-model",
+            r"aws\s+apigateway\s+delete-model\b",
+            "aws apigateway delete-model removes a model from an API."
+        ),
+        destructive_pattern!(
+            "apigateway-delete-domain-name",
+            r"aws\s+apigateway\s+delete-domain-name\b",
+            "aws apigateway delete-domain-name removes a custom domain name."
+        ),
+        destructive_pattern!(
+            "apigateway-delete-usage-plan",
+            r"aws\s+apigateway\s+delete-usage-plan\b",
+            "aws apigateway delete-usage-plan removes a usage plan."
+        ),
+        // HTTP API (APIGatewayV2) - destructive operations
+        destructive_pattern!(
+            "apigatewayv2-delete-api",
+            r"aws\s+apigatewayv2\s+delete-api\b",
+            "aws apigatewayv2 delete-api permanently removes an HTTP API."
+        ),
+        destructive_pattern!(
+            "apigatewayv2-delete-route",
+            r"aws\s+apigatewayv2\s+delete-route\b",
+            "aws apigatewayv2 delete-route removes a route from an HTTP API."
+        ),
+        destructive_pattern!(
+            "apigatewayv2-delete-integration",
+            r"aws\s+apigatewayv2\s+delete-integration\b",
+            "aws apigatewayv2 delete-integration removes an integration from an HTTP API."
+        ),
+        destructive_pattern!(
+            "apigatewayv2-delete-stage",
+            r"aws\s+apigatewayv2\s+delete-stage\b",
+            "aws apigatewayv2 delete-stage removes a stage from an HTTP API."
+        ),
+        destructive_pattern!(
+            "apigatewayv2-delete-authorizer",
+            r"aws\s+apigatewayv2\s+delete-authorizer\b",
+            "aws apigatewayv2 delete-authorizer removes an authorizer from an HTTP API."
+        ),
+        destructive_pattern!(
+            "apigatewayv2-delete-domain-name",
+            r"aws\s+apigatewayv2\s+delete-domain-name\b",
+            "aws apigatewayv2 delete-domain-name removes a custom domain name from an HTTP API."
+        ),
+        destructive_pattern!(
+            "apigatewayv2-delete-route-response",
+            r"aws\s+apigatewayv2\s+delete-route-response\b",
+            "aws apigatewayv2 delete-route-response removes a route response from an HTTP API."
+        ),
+        destructive_pattern!(
+            "apigatewayv2-delete-integration-response",
+            r"aws\s+apigatewayv2\s+delete-integration-response\b",
+            "aws apigatewayv2 delete-integration-response removes an integration response."
+        ),
+    ]
+}

+    fn test_pack_creation() {

+        assert!(pack.keywords.contains(&"apigatewayv2"));

+//! API Gateway packs - protections for API gateway management platforms.
+//!
+//! Provides protection for:
+//! - AWS API Gateway (REST and HTTP APIs)
+//! - Kong API Gateway (planned)
+//! - Google Apigee (planned)
+
+pub mod aws;
